metric_metadata: {"description": "How relevant the response is to the question", "default": true}
query: What Linux distributions and versions can I convert to RHEL using Red Hat Insights?
response:
You can convert several Linux distributions to Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) using the `Convert2RHEL` utility. This command-line tool analyzes your system's readiness and converts RPM-based Linux operating systems to RHEL.

Specifically, `Convert2RHEL` supports converting:

*   **CentOS Linux** to RHEL.

While the search results directly mention CentOS Linux, `Convert2RHEL` is designed to convert **RPM-based Linux operating systems** to RHEL. This implies it can be used for other distributions within that category, though specific support may vary. Red Hat Satellite can extend the `Convert2RHEL` experience to manage conversions at scale.

**Sources:**
- [How to Convert to RHEL from CentOS](https://access.redhat.com/articles/how-to-convert-to-rhel-from-centos)

- Rocky Linux 8 → RHEL 8

Before converting, run `convert2rhel analyze` to verify your system is eligible.

query: What Linux distributions and versions can I convert to RHEL using Red Hat Insights?
response:
You can convert several Linux distributions to Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) using the `Convert2RHEL` utility. This command-line tool analyzes your system's readiness and converts RPM-based Linux operating systems to RHEL.

Specifically, `Convert2RHEL` supports converting:

*   **CentOS Linux** to RHEL.

While the search results directly mention CentOS Linux, `Convert2RHEL` is designed to convert **RPM-based Linux operating systems** to RHEL. This implies it can be used for other distributions within that category, though specific support may vary. Red Hat Satellite can extend the `Convert2RHEL` experience to manage conversions at scale.

**Sources:**
- [How to Convert to RHEL from CentOS](https://access.redhat.com/articles/how-to-convert-to-rhel-from-centos)

⚠️ Deprecation/Removal Notice
**rhel_system_roles fails to apply kernel tuning settings using tuned**
Type: Solution | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7137347
Content: To resolve the issue: Remove all previously installed versions of the redhat.rhel_system_roles collection. Ensure that only version 1.108.6 is installed from Red Hat Automation Hub. Verify that no RPM-provided system roles are being unintentionally used. Re-run the playbook. After ensuring that only the correct collection version is available, the playbook should execute successfully without invoking the deprecated internal kernel_settings module. Root Cause The error is caused by the use of an older version of the kernel_settings role that relied on a deprecated internal Ansible module named kernel_settings (* Note: both a role and a module existed with the same name).

**How to uninstall Red Hat Satellite 6?**
Type: Solution | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/3414461
Content: Resolution For Red Hat Satellite 6.6 or older versions, there was a script katello-remove that - after two confirmation of two warning messages - removed the installed packages and dropped databases. Due to the non-trivial maintenance of the script, it was decided to drop it from Satellite deployment and uninstall Satellite instance by reinstalling the OS from scratch. Refer to  Bugzilla # 1806548 for more information. For further details, the official documentation for the particular version of Red Hat Satellite wrt. katello-remove can be referred to:-  Red Hat Satellite 6.0 - Red Hat Satellite 6.1 - Red Hat Satellite 6.2 - Red Hat Satellite 6.3 - Red Hat Satellite 6.4 - Red Hat Satellite 6.5 - Red Hat Satellite 6.6 For Red Hat Satellite 6.7 and newer versions , the katello-remove script has been deprecated and is no longer available.

**rhel-system-roles is removing interface defined static routes.**
Type: Solution | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/3347161
Content: rhel-system-roles is removing interface defined static routes. Solution Unverified - Updated 14 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) 7 Issue After configuring existing interface with rhel-system-roles the /etc/sysconfig/network-scripts/route-<interface> is no longer present. After configuring existing interface with rhel-system-roles the /etc/sysconfig/network-scripts/route6-<interface> is no longer present. Resolution Install rhel-system-roles-0.6-1.el7 via RHBA-2018:0439 With manual/static routes now supported, enable the route_append_only option & leave the routes list empty to prevent any modification to the route-<interface> & route6-<interface> files. ip: route_append_only: True dhcp4: no auto6: no address: - 10.0.0.1/24 Root Cause Previously rhel-system-roles did not support configuring manual/static routes. Thus when configuring an interface it would remove these files to ensure properly configured interface. Diagnostic Steps Upstream GitHub discussion: Content from github.com is not included. support for configuring static routes (th/static routes) Content from github.com is not included. commit 5e29382 library: merge branch 'th/static-routes' Upstream Documentation: < Content from github.com is not included. https://github.com/linux-system-roles/network > Tracked in Red Hat  Bugzilla 1487747

", "

**rhel_system_roles fails to apply kernel tuning settings using tuned**
Type: Solution | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7137347
Content: To resolve the issue: Remove all previously installed versions of the redhat.rhel_system_roles collection. Ensure that only version 1.108.6 is installed from Red Hat Automation Hub. Verify that no RPM-provided system roles are being unintentionally used. Re-run the playbook. After ensuring that only the correct collection version is available, the playbook should execute successfully without invoking the deprecated internal kernel_settings module. Root Cause The error is caused by the use of an older version of the kernel_settings role that relied on a deprecated internal Ansible module named kernel_settings (* Note: both a role and a module existed with the same name).

", "

**How to uninstall Red Hat Satellite 6?**
Type: Solution | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/3414461
Content: Resolution For Red Hat Satellite 6.6 or older versions, there was a script katello-remove that - after two confirmation of two warning messages - removed the installed packages and dropped databases. Due to the non-trivial maintenance of the script, it was decided to drop it from Satellite deployment and uninstall Satellite instance by reinstalling the OS from scratch. Refer to  Bugzilla # 1806548 for more information. For further details, the official documentation for the particular version of Red Hat Satellite wrt. katello-remove can be referred to:-  Red Hat Satellite 6.0 - Red Hat Satellite 6.1 - Red Hat Satellite 6.2 - Red Hat Satellite 6.3 - Red Hat Satellite 6.4 - Red Hat Satellite 6.5 - Red Hat Satellite 6.6 For Red Hat Satellite 6.7 and newer versions , the katello-remove script has been deprecated and is no longer available.

query: What is the correct rpm-ostree command to view the list of deployments on a system?
response:
To view the list of deployments on a system using `rpm-ostree`, use the `rpm-ostree status` command:

```bash
rpm-ostree status
```

This command will show you the current and previous deployments, their versions, and other relevant information.

**How to edit kernel options on an rpm-ostree/bootc system**
Type: Solution | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7069583
Content: How to edit kernel options on an rpm-ostree/bootc system Solution Unverified - Updated 17 May 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9bootcrpm-ostreeRHEL for edge Issue How to edit kernel options on an rpm-ostree/bootc system Resolution Kernel options can be added or taken away using the rpm-ostree kargs command. # rpm-ostree kargs --helpUsage: rpm-ostree kargs [OPTION…]Query or modify kernel argumentsApplication Options: --append=KEY=VALUE Append kernel argument; useful with e.g. console= that can be used multiple times. empty value for an argument is allowed --replace=KEY=VALUE=NEWVALUE Replace existing kernel argument, the user is also able to replace an argument with KEY=VALUE if only one value exist for that argument --delete=KEY=VALUE Delete a specific kernel argument key/val pair or an entire argument with a single key/value pair --append-if-missing=KEY=VALUE Like --append, but does nothing if the key is already present --delete-if-present=KEY=VALUE Like --delete, but does nothing if the key is already missing --editor Use an editor to modify the kernel arguments Example: # rpm-ostree kargs --append debug Staging deployment... doneFreed: 40.1 MB (pkgcache branches: 0)Changes queued for next boot. Run "systemctl reboot" to start a reboot This has been requested for official documentation via  [RHELDOCS-18175] How to edit kernel arguments in rpm-ostree system - Red Hat Issue Tracker

**How to edit bond connections configured via kernel command line for RHCOS nodes?**
Type: Solution | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7099123
Content: During upgrade from RHEL 8 to RHEL 9 (4.12 --> 4.13 OCP) Resolution Occasionally, an incorrect or outdated bonding configuration in grub.cfg can cause RCHOS node to not come with functional network . The following next steps show how to manually modify the boot parameter to fix the problem and let the system boot successfully with networking up. When the list of kernels appears at the beginning of the boot process, select the kernel to boot using the up/down arrow keys. Press key e to edit this entry. Use the arrow keys to move to the linux line. Append or change any necessary kernel options.Mention the right nic names , example (enp34s0f0np0,enp34s0f1np1) Press Ctrl-x to boot the system with the modified kernel options. If the system boots properly, the changes will need to be saved in the grub configuration file. Execute the below command to modify the kernel command line permanently. Below is an example, mention the nic names and network configuration as per your environment. # rpm-ostree kargs --delete="bond=bond0:enp34s0f0,enp34s0f1:mode=802.3ad,miimon=100"Staging deployment... doneChanges queued for next boot. Run "systemctl reboot" to start a reboot# rpm-ostree kargs --append="bond=bond0:enp34s0f0np0,enp34s0f1np1:mode=802.3ad,miimon=100"Staging deployment... doneChanges queued for next boot. Run "systemctl reboot" to start a reboot# systemctl reboot

Type: Solution | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/5694181
Content: Snip from logs $ sudo atomic host upgradeImporting metadata [=============] 100%ver-rpms Resolving dependencies... Forbidden base package replacements: kernel 3.10.0-1160.11.1.el7 -> 3.10.0-1127.18.2.el7 (rhel-7-server-rpms)failederror: Some base packages would be replaced Resolution Check for any packages shown in InactiveRequests list in rpm-ostree status -v output. Remove the additional layer(ie the package) using the command rpm-ostree uninstall <package shown in InactiveRequests> . This will not remove the base package but just removes the erroneous additional layer. After removing the package, reboot the node. Root Cause In the below rpm-ostree status -v output, kernel-3.10.0-1127.18.2.el7.x86_64 is listed as InactiveRequests . This additional layer was added using the command rpm-ostree install kernel-3.10.0-1127.18.2.el7.x86_64 . Since RHEL Atomic host 7.8.3 already has the kernel kernel-3.10.0-1127.18.2.el7.x86_64 as the base package, it is safe to remove the kernel and reboot the node. $ sudo rpm-ostree status -vState: idle; auto updates disabledDeployments:* ostree://rhel-atomic-host-ostree:rhel-atomic-host/7/x86_64/standard Version: 7.8.3 (2020-08-05 13:51:05) Commit: dfd383553c0f25c503272b0d193ac863f2deede0fa69278391bd2b1e6d02b56a `- atomic-rhel-7.8 (2020-06-23 09:43:12) `- ceph-tools-2 (2020-06-02 09:11:02) `- glusterfs (2020-07-23 06:46:54) `- rhel-7.8-server-extras (2020-06-23 09:33:41) `- rhel-7.8-server-optional (2020-08-03 09:09:02) `- rhel-7.8-server (2020 [...]

**Machine Config Operator Degraded - unexpected on-disk state validating against rendered-worker: Missing expected kernel arguments in OCP 4.13**
Type: Solution | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7049418
Content: Following the next steps should fix the issue: Check the node(s) and the MachineConfig(s) facing the issue as explained in the Diagnostic Steps section. Run the below commands on the affected nodes (as root) where the error is seen: $ rpm-ostree kargs$ rpm-ostree status --json$ rpm-ostree kargs --append-if-missing=systemd.unified_cgroup_hierarchy=0 --append-if-missing=systemd.legacy_systemd_cgroup_controller=1 --unchanged-exit-77$ echo $?$ rpm-ostree status --json$ rpm-ostree kargs$ systemctl reboot After the node has rebooted, review the config: $ cat /proc/cmdline$ cat /boot/loader/entries/ostree-1-rhcos.conf$ rpm-ostree status --json Root Cause RHOCP 4.13 is based on Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) 9.2 and hence need to add the kernel parameters to the system which uses custom MCPs."]
expected_response: The correct command is `rpm-ostree status`. This command is used to view rpm-ostree deployments and system status on rpm-ostree-based systems such as RHEL CoreOS and OpenShift Container Platform nodes. The command displays information about the current deployment state, including the booted deployment, any staged deployments, and available rollback deployments.

**How to edit kernel options on an rpm-ostree/bootc system**
Type: Solution | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7069583
Content: How to edit kernel options on an rpm-ostree/bootc system Solution Unverified - Updated 17 May 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9bootcrpm-ostreeRHEL for edge Issue How to edit kernel options on an rpm-ostree/bootc system Resolution Kernel options can be added or taken away using the rpm-ostree kargs command. # rpm-ostree kargs --helpUsage: rpm-ostree kargs [OPTION…]Query or modify kernel argumentsApplication Options: --append=KEY=VALUE Append kernel argument; useful with e.g. console= that can be used multiple times. empty value for an argument is allowed --replace=KEY=VALUE=NEWVALUE Replace existing kernel argument, the user is also able to replace an argument with KEY=VALUE if only one value exist for that argument --delete=KEY=VALUE Delete a specific kernel argument key/val pair or an entire argument with a single key/value pair --append-if-missing=KEY=VALUE Like --append, but does nothing if the key is already present --delete-if-present=KEY=VALUE Like --delete, but does nothing if the key is already missing --editor Use an editor to modify the kernel arguments Example: # rpm-ostree kargs --append debug Staging deployment... doneFreed: 40.1 MB (pkgcache branches: 0)Changes queued for next boot. Run "systemctl reboot" to start a reboot This has been requested for official documentation via  [RHELDOCS-18175] How to edit kernel arguments in rpm-ostree system - Red Hat Issue Tracker

Type: Solution | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/3909051
Content: How to pass a kernel parameter and persist it with Red Hat Enterprise Linux Atomic Host Solution Verified - Updated 14 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux Atomic Host 7 Issue How to pass a kernel parameter and persist it with Red Hat Enterprise Linux Atomic Host? Resolution Use command: rpm-ostree ex kargs --append=key=value The same settings can be removed with: rpm-ostree kargs --delete=key=value The current to be applied kernel command line can be seen with: $ rpm-ostree kargsrhcos.root=crypt_rootfs console=tty0 console=ttyS0,115200n8 ignition.platform.id=metal rd.luks.options=discard $ignition_firstboot ostree=/ostree/boot.1/rhcos/5d28cf40a6d5b0d4812f3ed4d9bfbecd40f67a29e1a70c07657c65097a8f1979/0 For further options, see the online help: $ rpm-ostree kargs --helpUsage: rpm-ostree kargs [OPTION…] Query or modify kernel argumentsHelp Options: -h, --help Show help optionsApplication Options: --os=OSNAME Operation on provided OSNAME --deploy-index=INDEX Modify the kernel args from a specific deployment based on index. Index is in the form of a number (e.g. 0 means the first deployment in the list) --reboot Initiate a reboot after operation is complete --append=KEY=VALUE Append kernel argument; useful with e.g. console= that can be used multiple times. empty value for an argument is allowed --replace=KEY=VALUE=NEWVALUE Replace existing kernel argument, the user is also able to replace an argument with KEY=VALUE if only one value exist for that argument --delete=KEY=V [...]

**How to edit kernel options on an rpm-ostree/bootc system**
Type: Solution | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7069583
Content: How to edit kernel options on an rpm-ostree/bootc system Solution Unverified - Updated 17 May 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9bootcrpm-ostreeRHEL for edge Issue How to edit kernel options on an rpm-ostree/bootc system Resolution Kernel options can be added or taken away using the rpm-ostree kargs command. # rpm-ostree kargs --helpUsage: rpm-ostree kargs [OPTION…]Query or modify kernel argumentsApplication Options: --append=KEY=VALUE Append kernel argument; useful with e.g. console= that can be used multiple times. empty value for an argument is allowed --replace=KEY=VALUE=NEWVALUE Replace existing kernel argument, the user is also able to replace an argument with KEY=VALUE if only one value exist for that argument --delete=KEY=VALUE Delete a specific kernel argument key/val pair or an entire argument with a single key/value pair --append-if-missing=KEY=VALUE Like --append, but does nothing if the key is already present --delete-if-present=KEY=VALUE Like --delete, but does nothing if the key is already missing --editor Use an editor to modify the kernel arguments Example: # rpm-ostree kargs --append debug Staging deployment... doneFreed: 40.1 MB (pkgcache branches: 0)Changes queued for next boot. Run "systemctl reboot" to start a reboot This has been requested for official documentation via  [RHELDOCS-18175] How to edit kernel arguments in rpm-ostree system - Red Hat Issue Tracker

", "

**How to edit bond connections configured via kernel command line for RHCOS nodes?**
Type: Solution | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7099123
Content: During upgrade from RHEL 8 to RHEL 9 (4.12 --> 4.13 OCP) Resolution Occasionally, an incorrect or outdated bonding configuration in grub.cfg can cause RCHOS node to not come with functional network . The following next steps show how to manually modify the boot parameter to fix the problem and let the system boot successfully with networking up. When the list of kernels appears at the beginning of the boot process, select the kernel to boot using the up/down arrow keys. Press key e to edit this entry. Use the arrow keys to move to the linux line. Append or change any necessary kernel options.Mention the right nic names , example (enp34s0f0np0,enp34s0f1np1) Press Ctrl-x to boot the system with the modified kernel options. If the system boots properly, the changes will need to be saved in the grub configuration file. Execute the below command to modify the kernel command line permanently. Below is an example, mention the nic names and network configuration as per your environment. # rpm-ostree kargs --delete="bond=bond0:enp34s0f0,enp34s0f1:mode=802.3ad,miimon=100"Staging deployment... doneChanges queued for next boot. Run "systemctl reboot" to start a reboot# rpm-ostree kargs --append="bond=bond0:enp34s0f0np0,enp34s0f1np1:mode=802.3ad,miimon=100"Staging deployment... doneChanges queued for next boot. Run "systemctl reboot" to start a reboot# systemctl reboot

**Manually Verifying OS Trees**
Type: Article | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/articles/1366233
Content: Manually Verifying OS Trees Updated 11 Apr 2017 Introduction The `sudo rpm-ostree upgrade` command uses pinned SSL certificates to retrieve new trees. Users who want to manually verify the provenance of a tree before switching to it can use the following straightforward procedure. Prerequisites Atomic These instructions assume that you want to verify an OSTree from within an instance of Atomic itself (from within the Atomic host instance). The following instructions can be run by Atomic's default user, and requires no additional installation of software.

**Why atomic host upgrade tries to replace the base kernel package on Red Hat Enterprise Atomic Host ?**
Type: Solution | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/5694181
Content: Snip from logs $ sudo atomic host upgradeImporting metadata [=============] 100%ver-rpms Resolving dependencies... Forbidden base package replacements: kernel 3.10.0-1160.11.1.el7 -> 3.10.0-1127.18.2.el7 (rhel-7-server-rpms)failederror: Some base packages would be replaced Resolution Check for any packages shown in InactiveRequests list in rpm-ostree status -v output. Remove the additional layer(ie the package) using the command rpm-ostree uninstall <package shown in InactiveRequests> . This will not remove the base package but just removes the erroneous additional layer. After removing the package, reboot the node. Root Cause In the below rpm-ostree status -v output, kernel-3.10.0-1127.18.2.el7.x86_64 is listed as InactiveRequests . This additional layer was added using the command rpm-ostree install kernel-3.10.0-1127.18.2.el7.x86_64 . Since RHEL Atomic host 7.8.3 already has the kernel kernel-3.10.0-1127.18.2.el7.x86_64 as the base package, it is safe to remove the kernel and reboot the node. $ sudo rpm-ostree status -vState: idle; auto updates disabledDeployments:* ostree://rhel-atomic-host-ostree:rhel-atomic-host/7/x86_64/standard Version: 7.8.3 (2020-08-05 13:51:05) Commit: dfd383553c0f25c503272b0d193ac863f2deede0fa69278391bd2b1e6d02b56a `- atomic-rhel-7.8 (2020-06-23 09:43:12) `- ceph-tools-2 (2020-06-02 09:11:02) `- glusterfs (2020-07-23 06:46:54) `- rhel-7.8-server-extras (2020-06-23 09:33:41) `- rhel-7.8-server-optional (2020-08-03 09:09:02) `- rhel-7.8-server (2020 [...]

**Machine Config Operator Degraded - unexpected on-disk state validating against rendered-worker: Missing expected kernel arguments in OCP 4.13**
Type: Solution | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7049418
Content: Following the next steps should fix the issue: Check the node(s) and the MachineConfig(s) facing the issue as explained in the Diagnostic Steps section. Run the below commands on the affected nodes (as root) where the error is seen: $ rpm-ostree kargs$ rpm-ostree status --json$ rpm-ostree kargs --append-if-missing=systemd.unified_cgroup_hierarchy=0 --append-if-missing=systemd.legacy_systemd_cgroup_controller=1 --unchanged-exit-77$ echo $?$ rpm-ostree status --json$ rpm-ostree kargs$ systemctl reboot After the node has rebooted, review the config: $ cat /proc/cmdline$ cat /boot/loader/entries/ostree-1-rhcos.conf$ rpm-ostree status --json Root Cause RHOCP 4.13 is based on Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) 9.2 and hence need to add the kernel parameters to the system which uses custom MCPs."]
expected_response: The correct command is `rpm-ostree status`. This command is used to view rpm-ostree deployments and system status on rpm-ostree-based systems such as RHEL CoreOS and OpenShift Container Platform nodes. The command displays information about the current deployment state, including the booted deployment, any staged deployments, and available rollback deployments.

metric_metadata: {"description": "Is what we retrieved actually relevant to user query?", "default": true}
query: What is the correct rpm-ostree command to view the list of deployments on a system?
response:
To view the list of deployments on a system using `rpm-ostree`, use the `rpm-ostree status` command:

```bash
rpm-ostree status
```

This command will show you the current and previous deployments, their versions, and other relevant information.

Sources:
- [RHEL for Edge overview videos](https://access.redhat.com/articles/5719641)
- [Why atomic host upgrade tries to replace the base kernel package on Red Hat Enterprise Atomic Host ?](https://access.redhat.com/solutions/5694181)
- [Machine Config Operator Degraded - unexpected on-disk state validating against rendered-worker: Missing expected kernel arguments in OCP 4.13](https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7049418)

query: How do I install packages on a system using rpm-ostree?
response:
To install packages using `rpm-ostree`, you use the `rpm-ostree install` command. After installation, a system reboot is required for the changes to take effect.

For example, to install the `htop` package:

```bash
rpm-ostree install htop
systemctl reboot
```

Red Hat generally recommends using containers to extend the functionality of Atomic Host and other `rpm-ostree` based systems, rather than layering many additional packages directly with `rpm-ostree`. This approach helps maintain system immutability and simplifies updates.

**Why rpm-ostree install fails with the message "error: Unsupported path:"?**
Type: Solution | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/3154491
Content: Why rpm-ostree install fails with the message "error: Unsupported path:"? Solution Unverified - Updated 11 Mar 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux Atomic Host 7.2 RHEL-for-edge rpm-ostree install Issue Why rpm-ostree install fails with the message "error: Unsupported path:" ? What is the recommended way to install rpm on Atomic Host. The standard RPM installation fails with: [root@XXXX~]# rpm-ostree install XXXXX.rpmerror: Unsupported path: /opt/XXX; See https://github.com/projectatomic/rpm-ostree/issues/233 Resolution "rpm-ostree install" will not allow to place package binaries in /opt. The rpm package should be re-built so that it uses /usr/opt or /usr/local/opt etc. Allowed paths are /usr, /bin, /sbin, /lib and /lib64 Workaround: Build a container image to include the third party daemon or agent The other workaround is to unzip the rpm provided this rpm does not require to place any binaries in /usr or other read only locations within Atomic Host. Below step can be performed on any other hosts or in the Atomic Host itself 1 mkdir /root/rpm2 cp /path-to-rpm /root/rpm [Copy the rpm to /root/rpm]3 cd /var/rpm-puppet4 rpm2cpio XXX.rpm | cpio -imdf [unzip the rpm]5 Copy the files to /opt etc6 Perform any manual actions included in the rpm (rpm -q --scripts XXXX.rpm) Root Cause OSTree defines /opt (really /var/opt) as system administrator territory - it's never rolled forward/backwards etc. Content in there isn't protected by the ro bind mount covering /usr.

**How do I install additional packages on Atomic Host**
Type: Solution | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/3297891
Content: How do I install additional packages on Atomic Host Solution Verified - Updated 14 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux Atomic Host Issue How can additional or extra packages be installed on Atomic Host I want to add a package to the rpm-ostree branch Atomic Host is running Resolution Note that wherever possible, Red Hat strongly recommends the use of containers or system containers to extend the functionality of Atomic Host rather than adding packages to the rpm-ostree branch. It is possible however to add packages to the running branch by using rpm-ostree install $package . A restart of the Atomic Host system will be required after the package is installed so that the system will boot into the new branch."]
expected_response:
On rpm-ostree-based systems (RHEL Atomic Host, RHEL for Edge), install packages using:

```
rpm-ostree install <package_name>
```

**Important considerations:**

1. **Red Hat strongly recommends using containers or system containers** to extend functionality rather than adding packages to the rpm-ostree branch.

2. **Reboot required**: After installation, restart the system to boot into the new branch with the installed package.

3. **Path restrictions**: rpm-ostree will not install packages with binaries in `/opt`. Packages must use allowed paths: `/usr`, `/bin`, `/sbin`, `/lib`, and `/lib64`. Packages attempting to install to `/opt` will fail with "error: Unsupported path:"

**View pending changes:**
```
rpm-ostree status
```

While `rpm-ostree install` is the correct method for package installation on ostree-managed systems, containerization is the preferred approach for extending Atomic Host functionality.

**Why rpm-ostree install fails with the message "error: Unsupported path:"?**
Type: Solution | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/3154491
Content: Why rpm-ostree install fails with the message "error: Unsupported path:"? Solution Unverified - Updated 11 Mar 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux Atomic Host 7.2 RHEL-for-edge rpm-ostree install Issue Why rpm-ostree install fails with the message "error: Unsupported path:" ? What is the recommended way to install rpm on Atomic Host. The standard RPM installation fails with: [root@XXXX~]# rpm-ostree install XXXXX.rpmerror: Unsupported path: /opt/XXX; See https://github.com/projectatomic/rpm-ostree/issues/233 Resolution "rpm-ostree install" will not allow to place package binaries in /opt. The rpm package should be re-built so that it uses /usr/opt or /usr/local/opt etc. Allowed paths are /usr, /bin, /sbin, /lib and /lib64 Workaround: Build a container image to include the third party daemon or agent The other workaround is to unzip the rpm provided this rpm does not require to place any binaries in /usr or other read only locations within Atomic Host. Below step can be performed on any other hosts or in the Atomic Host itself 1 mkdir /root/rpm2 cp /path-to-rpm /root/rpm [Copy the rpm to /root/rpm]3 cd /var/rpm-puppet4 rpm2cpio XXX.rpm | cpio -imdf [unzip the rpm]5 Copy the files to /opt etc6 Perform any manual actions included in the rpm (rpm -q --scripts XXXX.rpm) Root Cause OSTree defines /opt (really /var/opt) as system administrator territory - it's never rolled forward/backwards etc. Content in there isn't protected by the ro bind mount covering /usr.

**Why rpm-ostree install fails with the message "error: Unsupported path:"?**
Type: Solution | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/3154491
Content: Why rpm-ostree install fails with the message "error: Unsupported path:"? Solution Unverified - Updated 11 Mar 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux Atomic Host 7.2 RHEL-for-edge rpm-ostree install Issue Why rpm-ostree install fails with the message "error: Unsupported path:" ? What is the recommended way to install rpm on Atomic Host. The standard RPM installation fails with: [root@XXXX~]# rpm-ostree install XXXXX.rpmerror: Unsupported path: /opt/XXX; See https://github.com/projectatomic/rpm-ostree/issues/233 Resolution "rpm-ostree install" will not allow to place package binaries in /opt. The rpm package should be re-built so that it uses /usr/opt or /usr/local/opt etc. Allowed paths are /usr, /bin, /sbin, /lib and /lib64 Workaround: Build a container image to include the third party daemon or agent The other workaround is to unzip the rpm provided this rpm does not require to place any binaries in /usr or other read only locations within Atomic Host. Below step can be performed on any other hosts or in the Atomic Host itself 1 mkdir /root/rpm2 cp /path-to-rpm /root/rpm [Copy the rpm to /root/rpm]3 cd /var/rpm-puppet4 rpm2cpio XXX.rpm | cpio -imdf [unzip the rpm]5 Copy the files to /opt etc6 Perform any manual actions included in the rpm (rpm -q --scripts XXXX.rpm) Root Cause OSTree defines /opt (really /var/opt) as system administrator territory - it's never rolled forward/backwards etc. Content in there isn't protected by the ro bind mount covering /usr.

**How do I install additional packages on Atomic Host**
Type: Solution | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/3297891
Content: How do I install additional packages on Atomic Host Solution Verified - Updated 14 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux Atomic Host Issue How can additional or extra packages be installed on Atomic Host I want to add a package to the rpm-ostree branch Atomic Host is running Resolution Note that wherever possible, Red Hat strongly recommends the use of containers or system containers to extend the functionality of Atomic Host rather than adding packages to the rpm-ostree branch. It is possible however to add packages to the running branch by using rpm-ostree install $package . A restart of the Atomic Host system will be required after the package is installed so that the system will boot into the new branch."]
expected_response:
On rpm-ostree-based systems (RHEL Atomic Host, RHEL for Edge), install packages using:

```
rpm-ostree install <package_name>
```

**Important considerations:**

1. **Red Hat strongly recommends using containers or system containers** to extend functionality rather than adding packages to the rpm-ostree branch.

2. **Reboot required**: After installation, restart the system to boot into the new branch with the installed package.

3. **Path restrictions**: rpm-ostree will not install packages with binaries in `/opt`. Packages must use allowed paths: `/usr`, `/bin`, `/sbin`, `/lib`, and `/lib64`. Packages attempting to install to `/opt` will fail with "error: Unsupported path:"

**View pending changes:**
```
rpm-ostree status
```

While `rpm-ostree install` is the correct method for package installation on ostree-managed systems, containerization is the preferred approach for extending Atomic Host functionality.

metric_metadata: {"description": "Is what we retrieved actually relevant to user query?", "default": true}
query: How do I install packages on a system using rpm-ostree?
response:
To install packages using `rpm-ostree`, you use the `rpm-ostree install` command. After installation, a system reboot is required for the changes to take effect.

For example, to install the `htop` package:

```bash
rpm-ostree install htop
systemctl reboot
```

Red Hat generally recommends using containers to extend the functionality of Atomic Host and other `rpm-ostree` based systems, rather than layering many additional packages directly with `rpm-ostree`. This approach helps maintain system immutability and simplifies updates.

Find and take note of the current machine image: $ oc get clusterversion version --no-headers | awk '{print $2}' | xargs oc adm release info --image-for=machine-os-content 2. Enter inside the node by creating a debug pod: $ oc debug node/<NODE> 3. Change the chroot to /host : $ chroot /host 4. Create the directory mco-machine-os-content/os-content/ in /run : # mkdir -p /run/mco-m [...]

**Why rpm-ostree install fails with the message "error: Unsupported path:"?**
Type: Solution | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/3154491
Content: Why rpm-ostree install fails with the message "error: Unsupported path:"? Solution Unverified - Updated 11 Mar 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux Atomic Host 7.2 RHEL-for-edge rpm-ostree install Issue Why rpm-ostree install fails with the message "error: Unsupported path:" ? What is the recommended way to install rpm on Atomic Host. The standard RPM installation fails with: [root@XXXX~]# rpm-ostree install XXXXX.rpmerror: Unsupported path: /opt/XXX; See https://github.com/projectatomic/rpm-ostree/issues/233 Resolution "rpm-ostree install" will not allow to place package binaries in /opt. The rpm package should be re-built so that it uses /usr/opt or /usr/local/opt etc. Allowed paths are /usr, /bin, /sbin, /lib and /lib64 Workaround: Build a container image to include the third party daemon or agent The other workaround is to unzip the rpm provided this rpm does not require to place any binaries in /usr or other read only locations within Atomic Host. Below step can be performed on any other hosts or in the Atomic Host itself 1 mkdir /root/rpm2 cp /path-to-rpm /root/rpm [Copy the rpm to /root/rpm]3 cd /var/rpm-puppet4 rpm2cpio XXX.rpm | cpio -imdf [unzip the rpm]5 Copy the files to /opt etc6 Perform any manual actions included in the rpm (rpm -q --scripts XXXX.rpm) Root Cause OSTree defines /opt (really /var/opt) as system administrator territory - it's never rolled forward/backwards etc. Content in there isn't protected by the ro bind mount covering /usr.

", "

**rpm-ostree start timeout on OCP 4.11**
Type: Solution | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6972394
Content: rpm-ostree start timeout on OCP 4.11 Solution Unverified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat OpenShift Container Platform 4.11 Issue Install of or upgrade to OCP 4.11 fails due to rpm-ostreed service start error systemd[1]: Starting rpm-ostree System Management Daemon...systemd[1]: rpm-ostreed.service: start operation timed out. Terminating.systemd[1]: rpm-ostreed.service: Failed with result 'timeout'.systemd[1]: Failed to start rpm-ostree System Management Daemon. Resolution Two workarounds currently exist. Either one should allow the rpm-ostreed.service to start, and from there the machine-config-daemon can progress Drain each node of container workloads to reduce both the CPU load on and the number of container filesystems on the node Manually override the rpm-ostreed.service to skip scanning the container filesystem paths Create the /etc/systemd/system/rpm-ostreed.service.d directory # mkdir -p /etc/systemd/system/rpm-ostreed.service.d Create the file /etc/systemd/system/rpm-ostreed.service.d/bug2111817.conf with the following contents: [Service]InaccessiblePaths= Restart systemd # systemctl daemon-reload Root Cause Nodes with substantial numbers of containers and contention for CPU time are likely to have the rpm-ostreed.service fail to start, and the machine-config-daemon will not be able to progress. This issue is being tracked in  Bug 2111817 - rpm-ostree start timeout when upgrade from 4.11.0-rc.5-aarch64 to 4.11.0-rc.6-aarch64 Diagnostic Steps Check rpm-ostree [...]

", "

**How do I install additional packages on Atomic Host**
Type: Solution | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/3297891
Content: How do I install additional packages on Atomic Host Solution Verified - Updated 14 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux Atomic Host Issue How can additional or extra packages be installed on Atomic Host I want to add a package to the rpm-ostree branch Atomic Host is running Resolution Note that wherever possible, Red Hat strongly recommends the use of containers or system containers to extend the functionality of Atomic Host rather than adding packages to the rpm-ostree branch. It is possible however to add packages to the running branch by using rpm-ostree install $package . A restart of the Atomic Host system will be required after the package is installed so that the system will boot into the new branch."]
expected_response:
On rpm-ostree-based systems (RHEL Atomic Host, RHEL for Edge), install packages using:

```
rpm-ostree install <package_name>
```

**Important considerations:**

1. **Red Hat strongly recommends using containers or system containers** to extend functionality rather than adding packages to the rpm-ostree branch.

2. **Reboot required**: After installation, restart the system to boot into the new branch with the installed package.

3. **Path restrictions**: rpm-ostree will not install packages with binaries in `/opt`. Packages must use allowed paths: `/usr`, `/bin`, `/sbin`, `/lib`, and `/lib64`. Packages attempting to install to `/opt` will fail with "error: Unsupported path:"

**View pending changes:**
```
rpm-ostree status
```

While `rpm-ostree install` is the correct method for package installation on ostree-managed systems, containerization is the preferred approach for extending Atomic Host functionality.

Content: Supported RHEL versions for Red Hat Update Infrastructure Solution Verified - Updated 25 Dec 2025 Environment Red Hat Update Infrastructure 4 Red Hat Update Infrastructure 5 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue Identifying which versions of Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) are supported as the base operating system when deploying RHUI. Resolution The supported RHEL version is determined by the major version of RHUI being deployed: Red Hat Update Infrastructure 4 Supported OS: RHEL 8Limitations: Installation of RHUA and CDS nodes on RHEL 9 or later is not supported.Exception: The HAProxy node for load balancing is the only component that can be deployed on RHEL 9. **rhel-system-roles is removing interface defined static routes.**
Type: Solution | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/3347161
Content: rhel-system-roles is removing interface defined static routes. Solution Unverified - Updated 14 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) 7 Issue After configuring existing interface with rhel-system-roles the /etc/sysconfig/network-scripts/route-<interface> is no longer present. After configuring existing interface with rhel-system-roles the /etc/sysconfig/network-scripts/route6-<interface> is no longer present. Resolution Install rhel-system-roles-0.6-1.el7 via RHBA-2018:0439 With manual/static routes now supported, enable the route_append_only option & leave the routes list empty to prevent any modification to the route-<interface> & route6-<interface> files. ip: route_append_only: True dhcp4: no auto6: no address: - 10.0.0.1/24 Root Cause Previously rhel-system-roles did not support configuring manual/static routes. Thus when configuring an interface it would remove these files to ensure properly configured interface. Diagnostic Steps Upstream GitHub discussion: Content from github.com is not included. support for configuring static routes (th/static routes) Content from github.com is not included. commit 5e29382 library: merge branch 'th/static-routes' Upstream Documentation: < Content from github.com is not included. https://github.com/linux-system-roles/network > Tracked in Red Hat  Bugzilla 1487747

", "

**RHEL System Roles playbooks fail when run on localhost**
Type: Solution | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6726561
Content: RHEL System Roles playbooks fail when run on localhost Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment RHEL 8.6 or later with Ansible Core Issue When running a playbook utilizing the implicit localhost that calls a RHEL System Role on RHEL 8.6 or later with Ansible Core, the playbook fails with messages similar to: Example 1: TASK [rhel-system-roles.storage : get required packages] *****************************************************************************************************An exception occurred during task execution."]
expected_response:
RHEL System Roles support varies by individual role and is not uniform across the entire collection. Each role has two support dimensions:

**Control Node** (where Ansible runs): Most roles require RHEL 8, 9, or 10.

**Remote Host Management** (systems being configured): Support varies significantly by role:

- **Established roles** (introduced in RHEL 7.6/8.0) support the widest range:
  - **network, kdump, timesync**: RHEL 6, 7, 8, 9, 10
  - **selinux, ssh, sshd**: RHEL 6, 7, 8, 9, 10
  - **storage**: RHEL 7, 8, 9, 10

- **Mid-generation roles** have more limited support:
  - **nbde_client, nbde_server, certificate, vpn, firewall**: RHEL 7, 8, 9, 10
  - **tlog, crypto_policies, fapolicyd**: RHEL 8, 9, 10

- **Newer roles** support only recent versions:
  - **keylime_server**: RHEL 9.1+ only
  - **aide**: RHEL 8, 9, 10

The complete support matrix with initial release versions for all roles is documented at https://access.redhat.com/articles/3050101. Users should always check the specific role's documentation for accurate version requirements, as claiming "all roles support RHEL 9.0+" is incorrect—many established roles support RHEL 6 and 7.

URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7101311
Content: We have learned that NTLM will no longer be supported from version 9 onwards as the package gssntlmssp is deprecated. Is there any other way we can achieve this ? We have two different domains for Active Directory (AD) and Exchange. Currently, NTLM is used with a service account that directly accesses the Exchange server. If we switch to Kerberos, which is key-based authentication, it will not support this use case. We would like to know if Red Hat v9 supports NTLM authentication ? Is the package gssntlmssp available on RHEL 9 ? Resolution The gssntlmssp package has been deprecated and removed from RHEL 9. Microsoft also deprecated the NTLM functionality that is being used in windows servers. All versions of NTLM, including LANMAN, NTLMv1, and NTLMv2, are no longer under active feature development and are deprecated.

**RHEL System Roles playbooks fail when run on localhost**
Type: Solution | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6726561
Content: RHEL System Roles playbooks fail when run on localhost Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment RHEL 8.6 or later with Ansible Core Issue When running a playbook utilizing the implicit localhost that calls a RHEL System Role on RHEL 8.6 or later with Ansible Core, the playbook fails with messages similar to: Example 1: TASK [rhel-system-roles.storage : get required packages] *****************************************************************************************************An exception occurred during task execution."]
expected_response:
RHEL System Roles support varies by individual role and is not uniform across the entire collection. Each role has two support dimensions:

**Control Node** (where Ansible runs): Most roles require RHEL 8, 9, or 10.

**Remote Host Management** (systems being configured): Support varies significantly by role:

- **Established roles** (introduced in RHEL 7.6/8.0) support the widest range:
  - **network, kdump, timesync**: RHEL 6, 7, 8, 9, 10
  - **selinux, ssh, sshd**: RHEL 6, 7, 8, 9, 10
  - **storage**: RHEL 7, 8, 9, 10

- **Mid-generation roles** have more limited support:
  - **nbde_client, nbde_server, certificate, vpn, firewall**: RHEL 7, 8, 9, 10
  - **tlog, crypto_policies, fapolicyd**: RHEL 8, 9, 10

- **Newer roles** support only recent versions:
  - **keylime_server**: RHEL 9.1+ only
  - **aide**: RHEL 8, 9, 10

The complete support matrix with initial release versions for all roles is documented at https://access.redhat.com/articles/3050101. Users should always check the specific role's documentation for accurate version requirements, as claiming "all roles support RHEL 9.0+" is incorrect—many established roles support RHEL 6 and 7.

query: How do I rollback my system to a previous version using rpm-ostree?
response:
To roll back your system to a previous version using `rpm-ostree`, you can use the `rpm-ostree rollback` command. This command reverts your system to the immediately previous successful deployment.

1.  To initiate the rollback, execute the following command:
    ```bash
    rpm-ostree rollback
    ```
2.  After the rollback operation completes, a reboot is required for the changes to take effect:
    ```bash
    systemctl reboot
    ```

This will switch your system to the prior deployment.

**Sources:**
- [RHEL for Edge overview videos](https://access.redhat.com/articles/5719641)

Type: Solution | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6327541
Content: Machine Config Pool is degraded with error "Marking Degraded due to: error running rpm-ostree update --uninstall kernel-xxxx --uninstall kernel-xxxx: exit status 1" Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat OpenShift Container Platform (RHOCP) 4 Issue kernel-xxxx extension failed to install on a worker node. Rolling back to the previous render file failed with the error Package/capability 'kernel-xxxx' is not currently requested . Machine Config Pool (MCP) is stuck in a degraded state due to error: error running rpm-ostree update uninstall kernel-xxxx kernel-xxxx: exit status 1 (or error running rpm-ostree update --uninstall kernel-xxxx --uninstall kernel-xxxx: exit status 1 ). Resolution The following workaround is suggested: force a manual install of the "kernel-xxxx" package(s) then, the Machine Config Daemon (MCD) will automatically uninstall it (in order to reconcile the node with the rendered Machine Config (MC) , which doesn't include the additional package(s)) the Machine Config Pool (MCP) will be back to the Available state To execute such workaround, follow these steps: 1. Find and take note of the current machine image: $ oc get clusterversion version --no-headers | awk '{print $2}' | xargs oc adm release info --image-for=machine-os-content 2. Enter inside the node by creating a debug pod: $ oc debug node/<NODE> 3. Change the chroot to /host : $ chroot /host 4. Create the directory mco-machine-os-content/os-content/ in /run : # mkdir -p /run/mco-m [...]

**Machine Config Operator Degraded - unexpected on-disk state validating against rendered-worker: Missing expected kernel arguments in OCP 4.13**
Type: Solution | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7049418
Content: Following the next steps should fix the issue: Check the node(s) and the MachineConfig(s) facing the issue as explained in the Diagnostic Steps section. Run the below commands on the affected nodes (as root) where the error is seen: $ rpm-ostree kargs$ rpm-ostree status --json$ rpm-ostree kargs --append-if-missing=systemd.unified_cgroup_hierarchy=0 --append-if-missing=systemd.legacy_systemd_cgroup_controller=1 --unchanged-exit-77$ echo $?$ rpm-ostree status --json$ rpm-ostree kargs$ systemctl reboot After the node has rebooted, review the config: $ cat /proc/cmdline$ cat /boot/loader/entries/ostree-1-rhcos.conf$ rpm-ostree status --json Root Cause RHOCP 4.13 is based on Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) 9.2 and hence need to add the kernel parameters to the system which uses custom MCPs.

", "

**Unable to delete specific hugepages entries from '/proc/cmdline' in RHOCP 4**
Type: Solution | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7101276
Content: Unable to delete specific hugepages entries from '/proc/cmdline' in RHOCP 4 Solution Verified - Updated 3 Apr 2025 Environment Red Hat OpenShift Container Platform (RHOCP 4) Issue Missing tuned profile prevents kernel argument changes from applying to sysctl settings, causing configuration mismatch. Restarting the tuned pod does not remove hugepages kernel parameters after deleting the profile. Resolution From the node which is affected, run the below command which removes the hugepages=260 kernel boot parameter from the system's boot configuration using rpm-ostree : $ rpm-ostree kargs --delete="hugepages=260" A reboot is needed because kernel boot parameters are applied during the system's boot process: $ systemctl reboot **NOTE:**The arguments can be verified using "$ rpm-ostree kargs" and a pending ostree change is identified through "$ rpm-ostree status -vvv" .

", "

**How to edit kernel options on an rpm-ostree/bootc system**
Type: Solution | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7069583
Content: How to edit kernel options on an rpm-ostree/bootc system Solution Unverified - Updated 17 May 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9bootcrpm-ostreeRHEL for edge Issue How to edit kernel options on an rpm-ostree/bootc system Resolution Kernel options can be added or taken away using the rpm-ostree kargs command. # rpm-ostree kargs --helpUsage: rpm-ostree kargs [OPTION…]Query or modify kernel argumentsApplication Options: --append=KEY=VALUE Append kernel argument; useful with e.g. console= that can be used multiple times. empty value for an argument is allowed --replace=KEY=VALUE=NEWVALUE Replace existing kernel argument, the user is also able to replace an argument with KEY=VALUE if only one value exist for that argument --delete=KEY=VALUE Delete a specific kernel argument key/val pair or an entire argument with a single key/value pair --append-if-missing=KEY=VALUE Like --append, but does nothing if the key is already present --delete-if-present=KEY=VALUE Like --delete, but does nothing if the key is already missing --editor Use an editor to modify the kernel arguments Example: # rpm-ostree kargs --append debug Staging deployment... doneFreed: 40.1 MB (pkgcache branches: 0)Changes queued for next boot. Run "systemctl reboot" to start a reboot This has been requested for official documentation via  [RHELDOCS-18175] How to edit kernel arguments in rpm-ostree system - Red Hat Issue Tracker"]
expected_response:
To rollback to a previous RHEL for Edge image using rpm-ostree:

1. Use `rpm-ostree rollback` to rollback to the previous RHEL for Edge image
2. Use `rpm-ostree status` to show which image will be booted by default
3. Reboot the system to activate the rollback

The `rpm-ostree status` command displays rpm-ostree deployments and shows which deployment is currently active and which will be the default on next boot.

Note: The commands 'rpm-ostree split --list' and 'rpm-ostree transaction rollback' are not valid rpm-ostree commands for performing rollbacks.

URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7101276
Content: Unable to delete specific hugepages entries from '/proc/cmdline' in RHOCP 4 Solution Verified - Updated 3 Apr 2025 Environment Red Hat OpenShift Container Platform (RHOCP 4) Issue Missing tuned profile prevents kernel argument changes from applying to sysctl settings, causing configuration mismatch. query: How do I rollback my system to a previous version using rpm-ostree?
response:
To roll back your system to a previous version using `rpm-ostree`, you can use the `rpm-ostree rollback` command. This command reverts your system to the immediately previous successful deployment.

1.  To initiate the rollback, execute the following command:
    ```bash
    rpm-ostree rollback
    ```
2.  After the rollback operation completes, a reboot is required for the changes to take effect:
    ```bash
    systemctl reboot
    ```

This will switch your system to the prior deployment.

**Sources:**
- [RHEL for Edge overview videos](https://access.redhat.com/articles/5719641)

Type: Solution | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6327541
Content: Machine Config Pool is degraded with error "Marking Degraded due to: error running rpm-ostree update --uninstall kernel-xxxx --uninstall kernel-xxxx: exit status 1" Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat OpenShift Container Platform (RHOCP) 4 Issue kernel-xxxx extension failed to install on a worker node. Rolling back to the previous render file failed with the error Package/capability 'kernel-xxxx' is not currently requested . Machine Config Pool (MCP) is stuck in a degraded state due to error: error running rpm-ostree update uninstall kernel-xxxx kernel-xxxx: exit status 1 (or error running rpm-ostree update --uninstall kernel-xxxx --uninstall kernel-xxxx: exit status 1 ). Resolution The following workaround is suggested: force a manual install of the "kernel-xxxx" package(s) then, the Machine Config Daemon (MCD) will automatically uninstall it (in order to reconcile the node with the rendered Machine Config (MC) , which doesn't include the additional package(s)) the Machine Config Pool (MCP) will be back to the Available state To execute such workaround, follow these steps: 1. Find and take note of the current machine image: $ oc get clusterversion version --no-headers | awk '{print $2}' | xargs oc adm release info --image-for=machine-os-content 2. Enter inside the node by creating a debug pod: $ oc debug node/<NODE> 3. Change the chroot to /host : $ chroot /host 4. Create the directory mco-machine-os-content/os-content/ in /run : # mkdir -p /run/mco-m [...]

Type: Solution | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7049418
Content: Following the next steps should fix the issue: Check the node(s) and the MachineConfig(s) facing the issue as explained in the Diagnostic Steps section. URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6327541
Content: Machine Config Pool is degraded with error "Marking Degraded due to: error running rpm-ostree update --uninstall kernel-xxxx --uninstall kernel-xxxx: exit status 1" Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat OpenShift Container Platform (RHOCP) 4 Issue kernel-xxxx extension failed to install on a worker node. Rolling back to the previous render file failed with the error Package/capability 'kernel-xxxx' is not currently requested . Machine Config Pool (MCP) is stuck in a degraded state due to error: error running rpm-ostree update uninstall kernel-xxxx kernel-xxxx: exit status 1 (or error running rpm-ostree update --uninstall kernel-xxxx --uninstall kernel-xxxx: exit status 1 ). Resolution The following workaround is suggested: force a manual install of the "kernel-xxxx" package(s) then, the Machine Config Daemon (MCD) will automatically uninstall it (in order to reconcile the node with the rendered Machine Config (MC) , which doesn't include the additional package(s)) the Machine Config Pool (MCP) will be back to the Available state To execute such workaround, follow these steps: 1. Find and take note of the current machine image: $ oc get clusterversion version --no-headers | awk '{print $2}' | xargs oc adm release info --image-for=machine-os-content 2. Enter inside the node by creating a debug pod: $ oc debug node/<NODE> 3. Change the chroot to /host : $ chroot /host 4. Create the directory mco-machine-os-content/os-content/ in /run : # mkdir -p /run/mco-m [...]

**Machine Config Operator Degraded - unexpected on-disk state validating against rendered-worker: Missing expected kernel arguments in OCP 4.13**
Type: Solution | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7049418
Content: Following the next steps should fix the issue: Check the node(s) and the MachineConfig(s) facing the issue as explained in the Diagnostic Steps section. Run the below commands on the affected nodes (as root) where the error is seen: $ rpm-ostree kargs$ rpm-ostree status --json$ rpm-ostree kargs --append-if-missing=systemd.unified_cgroup_hierarchy=0 --append-if-missing=systemd.legacy_systemd_cgroup_controller=1 --unchanged-exit-77$ echo $?$ rpm-ostree status --json$ rpm-ostree kargs$ systemctl reboot After the node has rebooted, review the config: $ cat /proc/cmdline$ cat /boot/loader/entries/ostree-1-rhcos.conf$ rpm-ostree status --json Root Cause RHOCP 4.13 is based on Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) 9.2 and hence need to add the kernel parameters to the system which uses custom MCPs.

**Unable to delete specific hugepages entries from '/proc/cmdline' in RHOCP 4**
Type: Solution | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7101276
Content: Unable to delete specific hugepages entries from '/proc/cmdline' in RHOCP 4 Solution Verified - Updated 3 Apr 2025 Environment Red Hat OpenShift Container Platform (RHOCP 4) Issue Missing tuned profile prevents kernel argument changes from applying to sysctl settings, causing configuration mismatch. Restarting the tuned pod does not remove hugepages kernel parameters after deleting the profile. Resolution From the node which is affected, run the below command which removes the hugepages=260 kernel boot parameter from the system's boot configuration using rpm-ostree : $ rpm-ostree kargs --delete="hugepages=260" A reboot is needed because kernel boot parameters are applied during the system's boot process: $ systemctl reboot **NOTE:**The arguments can be verified using "$ rpm-ostree kargs" and a pending ostree change is identified through "$ rpm-ostree status -vvv" .

**How to edit kernel options on an rpm-ostree/bootc system**
Type: Solution | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7069583
Content: How to edit kernel options on an rpm-ostree/bootc system Solution Unverified - Updated 17 May 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9bootcrpm-ostreeRHEL for edge Issue How to edit kernel options on an rpm-ostree/bootc system Resolution Kernel options can be added or taken away using the rpm-ostree kargs command. # rpm-ostree kargs --helpUsage: rpm-ostree kargs [OPTION…]Query or modify kernel argumentsApplication Options: --append=KEY=VALUE Append kernel argument; useful with e.g. console= that can be used multiple times. empty value for an argument is allowed --replace=KEY=VALUE=NEWVALUE Replace existing kernel argument, the user is also able to replace an argument with KEY=VALUE if only one value exist for that argument --delete=KEY=VALUE Delete a specific kernel argument key/val pair or an entire argument with a single key/value pair --append-if-missing=KEY=VALUE Like --append, but does nothing if the key is already present --delete-if-present=KEY=VALUE Like --delete, but does nothing if the key is already missing --editor Use an editor to modify the kernel arguments Example: # rpm-ostree kargs --append debug Staging deployment... doneFreed: 40.1 MB (pkgcache branches: 0)Changes queued for next boot. Run "systemctl reboot" to start a reboot This has been requested for official documentation via  [RHELDOCS-18175] How to edit kernel arguments in rpm-ostree system - Red Hat Issue Tracker"]
expected_response:
To rollback to a previous RHEL for Edge image using rpm-ostree:

1. Use `rpm-ostree rollback` to rollback to the previous RHEL for Edge image
2. Use `rpm-ostree status` to show which image will be booted by default
3. Reboot the system to activate the rollback

The `rpm-ostree status` command displays rpm-ostree deployments and shows which deployment is currently active and which will be the default on next boot.

Note: The commands 'rpm-ostree split --list' and 'rpm-ostree transaction rollback' are not valid rpm-ostree commands for performing rollbacks.

query: How do I rollback my system to a previous version using rpm-ostree?
response:
To roll back your system to a previous version using `rpm-ostree`, you can use the `rpm-ostree rollback` command. This command reverts your system to the immediately previous successful deployment.

1.  To initiate the rollback, execute the following command:
    ```bash
    rpm-ostree rollback
    ```
2.  After the rollback operation completes, a reboot is required for the changes to take effect:
    ```bash
    systemctl reboot
    ```

This will switch your system to the prior deployment.

**Sources:**
- [RHEL for Edge overview videos](https://access.redhat.com/articles/5719641)

Content: Machine Config Pool is degraded with error "Marking Degraded due to: error running rpm-ostree update --uninstall kernel-xxxx --uninstall kernel-xxxx: exit status 1" Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat OpenShift Container Platform (RHOCP) 4 Issue kernel-xxxx extension failed to install on a worker node. Rolling back to the previous render file failed with the error Package/capability 'kernel-xxxx' is not currently requested . Machine Config Pool (MCP) is stuck in a degraded state due to error: error running rpm-ostree update uninstall kernel-xxxx kernel-xxxx: exit status 1 (or error running rpm-ostree update --uninstall kernel-xxxx --uninstall kernel-xxxx: exit status 1 ). Resolution The following workaround is suggested: force a manual install of the "kernel-xxxx" package(s) then, the Machine Config Daemon (MCD) will automatically uninstall it (in order to reconcile the node with the rendered Machine Config (MC) , which doesn't include the additional package(s)) the Machine Config Pool (MCP) will be back to the Available state To execute such workaround, follow these steps: 1. Find and take note of the current machine image: $ oc get clusterversion version --no-headers | awk '{print $2}' | xargs oc adm release info --image-for=machine-os-content 2. Enter inside the node by creating a debug pod: $ oc debug node/<NODE> 3. Change the chroot to /host : $ chroot /host 4. Create the directory mco-machine-os-content/os-content/ in /run : # mkdir -p /run/mco-m [...]

", "

**Machine Config Operator Degraded - unexpected on-disk state validating against rendered-worker: Missing expected kernel arguments in OCP 4.13**
Type: Solution | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7049418
Content: Following the next steps should fix the issue: Check the node(s) and the MachineConfig(s) facing the issue as explained in the Diagnostic Steps section. Run the below commands on the affected nodes (as root) where the error is seen: $ rpm-ostree kargs$ rpm-ostree status --json$ rpm-ostree kargs --append-if-missing=systemd.unified_cgroup_hierarchy=0 --append-if-missing=systemd.legacy_systemd_cgroup_controller=1 --unchanged-exit-77$ echo $?$ rpm-ostree status --json$ rpm-ostree kargs$ systemctl reboot After the node has rebooted, review the config: $ cat /proc/cmdline$ cat /boot/loader/entries/ostree-1-rhcos.conf$ rpm-ostree status --json Root Cause RHOCP 4.13 is based on Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) 9.2 and hence need to add the kernel parameters to the system which uses custom MCPs.

", "

**Unable to delete specific hugepages entries from '/proc/cmdline' in RHOCP 4**
Type: Solution | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7101276
Content: Unable to delete specific hugepages entries from '/proc/cmdline' in RHOCP 4 Solution Verified - Updated 3 Apr 2025 Environment Red Hat OpenShift Container Platform (RHOCP 4) Issue Missing tuned profile prevents kernel argument changes from applying to sysctl settings, causing configuration mismatch. Restarting the tuned pod does not remove hugepages kernel parameters after deleting the profile. Resolution From the node which is affected, run the below command which removes the hugepages=260 kernel boot parameter from the system's boot configuration using rpm-ostree : $ rpm-ostree kargs --delete="hugepages=260" A reboot is needed because kernel boot parameters are applied during the system's boot process: $ systemctl reboot **NOTE:**The arguments can be verified using "$ rpm-ostree kargs" and a pending ostree change is identified through "$ rpm-ostree status -vvv" .

", "

**How to edit kernel options on an rpm-ostree/bootc system**
Type: Solution | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7069583
Content: How to edit kernel options on an rpm-ostree/bootc system Solution Unverified - Updated 17 May 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9bootcrpm-ostreeRHEL for edge Issue How to edit kernel options on an rpm-ostree/bootc system Resolution Kernel options can be added or taken away using the rpm-ostree kargs command. # rpm-ostree kargs --helpUsage: rpm-ostree kargs [OPTION…]Query or modify kernel argumentsApplication Options: --append=KEY=VALUE Append kernel argument; useful with e.g. console= that can be used multiple times. empty value for an argument is allowed --replace=KEY=VALUE=NEWVALUE Replace existing kernel argument, the user is also able to replace an argument with KEY=VALUE if only one value exist for that argument --delete=KEY=VALUE Delete a specific kernel argument key/val pair or an entire argument with a single key/value pair --append-if-missing=KEY=VALUE Like --append, but does nothing if the key is already present --delete-if-present=KEY=VALUE Like --delete, but does nothing if the key is already missing --editor Use an editor to modify the kernel arguments Example: # rpm-ostree kargs --append debug Staging deployment... doneFreed: 40.1 MB (pkgcache branches: 0)Changes queued for next boot. Run "systemctl reboot" to start a reboot This has been requested for official documentation via  [RHELDOCS-18175] How to edit kernel arguments in rpm-ostree system - Red Hat Issue Tracker"]
expected_response:
To rollback to a previous RHEL for Edge image using rpm-ostree:

1. Use `rpm-ostree rollback` to rollback to the previous RHEL for Edge image
2. Use `rpm-ostree status` to show which image will be booted by default
3. Reboot the system to activate the rollback

The `rpm-ostree status` command displays rpm-ostree deployments and shows which deployment is currently active and which will be the default on next boot.

Note: The commands 'rpm-ostree split --list' and 'rpm-ostree transaction rollback' are not valid rpm-ostree commands for performing rollbacks.

query: What yum repository is the convert2rhel utility available in?
response:
The `convert2rhel` utility is available in the official Red Hat repositories, accessible via the Red Hat Content Delivery Network (CDN) through Red Hat Subscription Manager (RHSM). You can install it using `yum` or `dnf`.

yum -y install convert2rhel

The failing repository would need to be used in place of that example for the purposes of local troubleshooting. Contact the upstream for the repository and request that teams assistance to resolve the issue Repositories managed by Red Hat are generally configured via the subscription-manager utility. **convert2rhel utility fails stating realtek kernel module is not supported in RHEL**
Type: Solution | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6084321
Content: convert2rhel utility fails stating realtek kernel module is not supported in RHEL Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment CentOS 7.7 RHEL7.9 Convert2rhel Issue convert2rhel utility fails stating realtek kernel module is not supported in RHEL Converting to RHEL 7.9 from CentOS fails due to unsupported kernel module:CRITICAL - The following kernel modules are not supported in RHEL:/lib/modules/3.10.0-1062.12.1.el7.x86_64/kernel/drivers/net/phy/realtek.ko.xzUninstall or disable them and run convert2rhel again to continue with the conversion. Resolution Update CentOS7.7 to CentOS7.9 with all packages updated to latest version that is available in the repositories from the system. Convert2rhel will perform conversion to latest RHEL7.9 release and will perform comparison of origninal OS with RHEL7.9 before starting actual conversion. Root Cause realtek module is not present in RHEL7.9 and even in centos 7.9 as loadable kernel module. In this particular scenario if one tries to update CentOS 7.7 to RHEL7.9 using convert2rhel, the realtek module is present in CentOS7.7 and even in RHEL7.7 as loadable module, however in RHEL7.9 even in CentOS7.9 this module is moved to inbuild kernel module. # rpm -qpl kernel-3.10.0-1062.12.1.el7.x86_64.rpm |grep realtek.ko/lib/modules/3.10.0-1062.12.1.el7.x86_64/kernel/drivers/net/phy/realtek.ko.xz/lib/modules/3.10.0-1062.12.1.el7.x86_64/kernel/drivers/usb/storage/ums-realtek.ko.xz/lib/modules/3.10.0-1062.12.1.el7.x86_64/kernel/sound/pci/ [...]

**Converting from a Linux distribution to RHEL using the Convert2RHEL utility - Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8**
Type: Documentation | Applicability: RHEL

**Converting from a Linux distribution to RHEL using the Convert2RHEL utility in Red Hat Lightspeed - Red Hat Lightspeed 1-latest**
Type: Documentation | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_lightspeed/1-latest/html-single/converting_from_a_linux_distribution_to_rhel_using_the_convert2rhel_utility_in_red_hat_lightspeed/index
Content: Converting from a Linux distribution to RHEL using the Convert2RHEL utility in Red Hat Lightspeed Preface Providing feedback on Red Hat documentation Key migration terminology 1. Supported conversion paths 2. Conversion methods 3. Planning a RHEL conversion 4. Preparation for a RHEL conversion by using Red Hat Lightspeed 4.1. Prerequisites 4.2. Preparing for a RHEL conversion using Red Hat Lightspeed 4.3. Preparing for a RHEL conversion with a proxy server 5."]
expected_response:
The convert2rhel utility is available in the `convert2rhel-for-rhel-X-rpms` repository (where X is your RHEL major version: 7, 8, or 9).

To enable it:

1. Download the repository configuration file:
```
curl -o /etc/yum.repos.d/convert2rhel.repo https://cdn-public.redhat.com/content/public/addon/dist/convert2rhel/server/7/7Server/x86_64/os/convert2rhel.repo
```

2. Install the package:
```
yum install convert2rhel
```

The repository is publicly accessible on Red Hat's CDN because convert2rhel converts non-RHEL systems (CentOS, Oracle Linux, AlmaLinux, Rocky Linux) to RHEL - users typically don't have RHEL subscriptions when they need this tool.

**Note**: Convert2rhel is NOT available in standard RHEL repositories like BaseOS or AppStream.

expected_response:
The convert2rhel utility is available in the `convert2rhel-for-rhel-X-rpms` repository (where X is your RHEL major version: 7, 8, or 9).

To enable it:

1. Download the repository configuration file:
```
curl -o /etc/yum.repos.d/convert2rhel.repo https://cdn-public.redhat.com/content/public/addon/dist/convert2rhel/server/7/7Server/x86_64/os/convert2rhel.repo
```

2. Install the package:
```
yum install convert2rhel
```

The repository is publicly accessible on Red Hat's CDN because convert2rhel converts non-RHEL systems (CentOS, Oracle Linux, AlmaLinux, Rocky Linux) to RHEL - users typically don't have RHEL subscriptions when they need this tool.

**Note**: Convert2rhel is NOT available in standard RHEL repositories like BaseOS or AppStream.

Type: Article | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/articles/4077451
Content: Troubleshooting YUM Repository Failures Updated 24 Apr 2019 Overview The yum utility will occasionally return errors when attempting to connect to repositories or retrieve their metadata. This article discusses various possible causes of these errors and how to address them. These errors generally will be reported as output similar to the following: # yum repolistUpdating Subscription Management repositories.<repo name> 0.0 B/s | 0 B 00:00Failed to synchronize cache for repo '<repoid>' Contact the upstream for the repository and request that teams assistance to resolve the issue Verify the repository configuration Temporarily disable the repository Permanently disable the repository Note: All of the examples below use an example primary repository. The failing repository would need to be used in place of that example for the purposes of local troubleshooting. Contact the upstream for the repository and request that teams assistance to resolve the issue Repositories managed by Red Hat are generally configured via the subscription-manager utility. In the event that these repositories are not functioning properly, please open a case with Red Hat Support to investigate the source of the failure.

", "

expected_response:
The convert2rhel utility is available in the `convert2rhel-for-rhel-X-rpms` repository (where X is your RHEL major version: 7, 8, or 9).

To enable it:

1. Download the repository configuration file:
```
curl -o /etc/yum.repos.d/convert2rhel.repo https://cdn-public.redhat.com/content/public/addon/dist/convert2rhel/server/7/7Server/x86_64/os/convert2rhel.repo
```

2. Install the package:
```
yum install convert2rhel
```

The repository is publicly accessible on Red Hat's CDN because convert2rhel converts non-RHEL systems (CentOS, Oracle Linux, AlmaLinux, Rocky Linux) to RHEL - users typically don't have RHEL subscriptions when they need this tool.

**Note**: Convert2rhel is NOT available in standard RHEL repositories like BaseOS or AppStream.

```bash
yum -y install convert2rhel

Content: Troubleshooting YUM Repository Failures Updated 24 Apr 2019 Overview The yum utility will occasionally return errors when attempting to connect to repositories or retrieve their metadata. This article discusses various possible causes of these errors and how to address them. These errors generally will be reported as output similar to the following: # yum repolistUpdating Subscription Management repositories.<repo name> 0.0 B/s | 0 B 00:00Failed to synchronize cache for repo '<repoid>' Contact the upstream for the repository and request that teams assistance to resolve the issue Verify the repository configuration Temporarily disable the repository Permanently disable the repository Note: All of the examples below use an example primary repository. The failing repository would need to be used in place of that example for the purposes of local troubleshooting. Contact the upstream for the repository and request that teams assistance to resolve the issue Repositories managed by Red Hat are generally configured via the subscription-manager utility. In the event that these repositories are not functioning properly, please open a case with Red Hat Support to investigate the source of the failure.

**convert2rhel utility fails stating realtek kernel module is not supported in RHEL**
Type: Solution | Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6084321
Content: convert2rhel utility fails stating realtek kernel module is not supported in RHEL Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment CentOS 7.7 RHEL7.9 Convert2rhel Issue convert2rhel utility fails stating realtek kernel module is not supported in RHEL Converting to RHEL 7.9 from CentOS fails due to unsupported kernel module:CRITICAL - The following kernel modules are not supported in RHEL:/lib/modules/3.10.0-1062.12.1.el7.x86_64/kernel/drivers/net/phy/realtek.ko.xzUninstall or disable them and run convert2rhel again to continue with the conversion. Resolution Update CentOS7.7 to CentOS7.9 with all packages updated to latest version that is available in the repositories from the system. Convert2rhel will perform conversion to latest RHEL7.9 release and will perform comparison of origninal OS with RHEL7.9 before starting actual conversion. Root Cause realtek module is not present in RHEL7.9 and even in centos 7.9 as loadable kernel module. In this particular scenario if one tries to update CentOS 7.7 to RHEL7.9 using convert2rhel, the realtek module is present in CentOS7.7 and even in RHEL7.7 as loadable module, however in RHEL7.9 even in CentOS7.9 this module is moved to inbuild kernel module. # rpm -qpl kernel-3.10.0-1062.12.1.el7.x86_64.rpm |grep realtek.ko/lib/modules/3.10.0-1062.12.1.el7.x86_64/kernel/drivers/net/phy/realtek.ko.xz/lib/modules/3.10.0-1062.12.1.el7.x86_64/kernel/drivers/usb/storage/ums-realtek.ko.xz/lib/modules/3.10.0-1062.12.1.el7.x86_64/kernel/sound/pci/ [...]
